text:
Summarize (Orchestrating the Discussion)

Discussing Solutions and Strategies

Begin the discussion with groups sharing the strategies that you noted in the Explore. This is a time to practice listening, responding, and questioning strategies among students. It is important that students learn various strategies to increase their flexibility and fluency in solving ratio and rate questions.

The strategies may include finding unit rates, making ratio tables, multiplying/dividing both quantities in a ratio (scaling) to find larger or smaller values as needed, and other informal strategies based on multiplicative reasoning.

Making the Mathematics Explicit

Pay special attention to how students convert units. Labeling will be very important.

Suggested Questions

  • In the What If...? , it says someone can walk backward at a rate of 3 mph. What does 3 mph mean?
  • What does that mean for walking in minutes?
  • So how do you determine how far someone can walk at that rate for 30 minutes?
  • Do you agree with Nathan that the Ferris wheel ride questions in Number Connections were really about unit rates?
  • Are there other examples in Number Connections or Variables and Patterns that are ratios?

• 3 miles per hour or 3 miles for every 1 hour.
  • You can walk 3 miles in 60 minutes.
  • Students will offer various strategies. For example, they might take 3 miles in 60 minutes and talk half of each quantity. That would be 1.5 miles in 30 minutes.
  • Yes, but we didn't realize it at the time
  • There are several. Answers will vary. You may need to remind students of context to job their memory. The bike tour in Variables and Patterns are cicadas and bagging snacks in Number Connections are some examples. (Time)

Now What Do Students Know

Ask students to reflect on the discussion and answer the Now What Do You Know? questions.

See answers to the problem for more information about the strategies and embedded mathematics.

Reflecting on Student Learning

  • Use the following questions to assess student understanding at the end of the lesson.
    • What evidence do I have that students understand the Now What Do You Know? question?
      • Where did my students get stuck?
      • What strategies did they use?
      • What breakthroughs did my students have today?
    • How will I use this to plan for tomorrow? For the next time I teach this lesson?
    • Where will I have the opportunity to reinforce these ideas as I continue through this unit? The next unit?
